elements.sent-2411-001: Histopathology is practice of determining disease from optical images of human tissue.
elements.sent-2411-002: After staining by special dyes like hematoxylin and eosin (HE) to highlight structures, pathologists examine the sample under a microscope to identify patterns of deviation from healthy tissues.
elements.sent-2411-003: Specific cellular and tissue patterns indicate severity of the disease and decide treatment options \cite{mills2012sternberg .}
elements.sent-2411-004: Although this process has been well-established over the last 125 years as the standard means to diagnose most diseases, especially cancer, staining remains labor-intensive and costly.
elements.sent-2411-005: These issues are exacerbated by the need for multiple staining as a single stain cannot reveal all information for accurate diagnosis.
elements.sent-2411-006: Recently, image-to-image translation (I2IT) methods have emerged as a promising alternative, generating realistic stained images from different stains or label-free (i.e., stain-free) modalities \cite{mayerich2015stain, de2021deep, liu2021unpaired, liu2022bci, zeng2022semi, lin2022unpaired, zhang2022mvfstain, boyd2022region, li2023adaptive, li2024virtual, chen2024pathological, wang2024mix, wei2024derestainer, liu2023vsgd, ma2023efficient, bayramoglu2017towards, rivenson2019phasestain, rivenson2019virtual, schnell2020all, shi2023unsupervised, wolflein2023hoechstgan .}
elements.sent-2411-007: Termed virtual staining (VS), this approach offers faster assessment of tissues, at lower cost and with simpler workflows, thereby streamlining medical care for patients.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{fig1.png} \caption{Hallucination examples. Symptoms can be transparent (left) or realistic (right). The latter is particularly challenging to identify as the hallucination is within the target data manifold.} \label{fig1}
elements.sent-2411-008: However, VS models are known to ``hallucinate'' (Fig. \ref{fig1 ), producing false histopathological patterns that can mislead clinicians.}
elements.sent-2411-009: This poses deadly risk in high-stake healthcare settings through misdiagnosis.
elements.sent-2411-010: This highlights the need for a hallucination detector (or monitor) to serve as a co-pilot to VS to guide clinicians away from ill-decisions and uphold VS trustworthiness.
elements.sent-2411-011: Despite its clinical value, this task received little attention, with just two recent noteworthy preprints \cite{huang2024autonomous, ounissi2024scalable addressing it.}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, we notice some shortcomings in detection validation (with limited scope or misguided protocols) and method (particularly on scalability and robustness), detailed in \S\ref{sec:2.2 .\footnote{Based on arXiv submissions prior to Oct.. 2024.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The former issue is critical as erroneous practices--even minor ones--can skew the objective evaluation of detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-014: The latter issue is critical because--(i) VS is applied to whole slide images (WSIs), which can range into TB sizes, and (ii) VS datasets are diverse, covering different organs, stain and label-free techniques, and I2IT methods.
elements.sent-2411-015: These challenges demand a detector with high throughput and versatility.
elements.sent-2411-016: In this work, we address the first issue by clearly establishing the detection problem and its evaluation (\S\ref{sec:3 ).}
elements.sent-2411-017: We explore hallucination causes to clarify what hallucination detection is and isn't, namely underscoring that it is neither an out-of-distribution (OOD) nor outlier problem.
elements.sent-2411-018: Instead, it must align with VS prediction.
elements.sent-2411-019: We then address the second issue by proposing a hallucination detector dubbed Neural Hallucination Precursor (NHP, \S\ref{sec:4 ).}
elements.sent-2411-020: Briefly, NHP performs a post-hoc search for the optimal marker in the VS model's embedded space by merging feature signals and self-tuning all relevant performance parameters.
elements.sent-2411-021: This meets the scalability and versatility requirements.
elements.sent-2411-022: We show in \S\ref{sec:5 that NHP performs well irrespective of organ type, source/target pair (encompassing HE; immunohistochemistry, IHC; immunofluorescence, IF; and label-free stimulated Raman scattering, SRS), and I2IT backbone (supervised and unsupervised).}
elements.sent-2411-023: We further contribute by introducing new research themes to the broader VS community--these include adversarial attacks, and the interplay (or lack thereof) between hallucination robustness and detection to highlight the dangers of solely reporting one type of safety metric.
elements.sent-2411-024: The I2IT paradigm of VS can be either supervised, using source/target pairs from multiplexing or label-free modalities \cite{bayramoglu2017towards, rivenson2019phasestain, rivenson2019virtual, schnell2020all, wolflein2023hoechstgan, de2021deep , or unsupervised, using unpaired sets \cite{zhang2022mvfstain, zeng2022semi, boyd2022region, liu2021unpaired, lin2022unpaired .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Supervised I2IT methods perform better but suffer from technological overhead.
elements.sent-2411-026: An emerging trend is to use adjacent tissue slices as source/target, meticulously registered \cite{li2024virtual, wang2024mix, wei2024derestainer, liu2022bci, li2023adaptive, chen2024pathological .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Their proximity introduces a degree of perceptual similarity, sometimes termed an ``inconsistent pair''; traditional supervised methods remain elusive due to pixel misalignment, but the inconsistent pair is accepted for full-reference evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-028: Overall, GANs are the dominant I2IT approach in VS, with Pix2Pix \cite{isola2017image in supervised settings and CycleGAN \cite{zhu2017unpaired in unsupervised ones.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: In this work, we consider both supervised and unsupervised GAN-based VS tasks.
elements.sent-2411-030: As mentioned later, we require at least inconsistent pairs to define hallucinations, limiting our latter experiments to inconsistently paired datasets, even though they are trained in an unsupervised manner.
elements.sent-2411-031: Two recent studies have tackled hallucination detection specifically for vs. Ref. \cite{huang2024autonomous performed cyclic translations between source (autofluorescence) and target (HE) domains to define a hallucination score for lung and kidney biopsies.}
elements.sent-2411-032: However, this approach incurs latency and is restricted to GANs yielding the inverse target-to-source model, requiring its additional training otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-033: Another study \cite{ounissi2024scalable used the discriminator to monitor HE-to-IHC VS in pediatric Crohn’s disease.}
elements.sent-2411-034: While these studies adopt anomaly detection methods exploiting the GAN framework \cite{zenati2018adversarially, sabokrou2018adversarially, schlegl2019f , we posit that hallucination precursors exist in the neural embeddings of the VS generator before their manifestation.}
elements.sent-2411-035: We show later that our NHP method built upon this premise is significantly more robust.
elements.sent-2411-036: Additionally, the validation of these studies are primarily visual or limited.
elements.sent-2411-037: For instance, \cite{huang2024autonomous distinguished between ``good'' and ``poor'' VS models based on training status--while hallucinations are more common in latter, well-trained models are not immune to them.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Similarly, flagging corrupted source images \cite{ounissi2024scalable may not reliably indicate hallucinations when the VS model is robust.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Recognizing these limitations, we aim to provide a more accurate and objective study.
elements.sent-2411-040: Apart from these studies, mainstream VS literature seeks to mitigate hallucinations by preserving pathological semantics \cite{liu2021unpaired, zeng2022semi, lin2022unpaired,boyd2022region, li2023adaptive, ma2023efficient, li2024virtual, chen2024pathological, wang2024mix, wei2024derestainer .}
elements.sent-2411-041: In contrast, our goal is to detect hallucinations, irrespective of frequency.
elements.sent-2411-042: These goals can be mutually inclusive, but our primary focus here is on detection.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig2.png} \caption{Hallucination causes. We confirm each factor by ablating specific components (bottom row) in the VS pipeline and observing the resultant drop in MS-SSIM. The adopted experiment is from \S\ref{sec:5}: VS of HE (target) from 4 SRS bands (source), trained by Pix2PixHD \cite{wang2018high} and evaluated over an ID test set. OOD and adversarial example details in \S\ref{sec:5.3}. Similar results were seen with PSNR and LPIPS.} \label{fig2}
elements.sent-2411-043: VS estimates the I2IT mapping function $G: S \rightarrow T$, for source and target domains $S$ and $T$.
elements.sent-2411-044: Let us denote a dataset $\mathcal{D}$ with marginal distributions $P_S$ and $P_T$ for $S$ and $T$.
elements.sent-2411-045: We apply empirical risk minimization (ERM) to find $\hat{G \triangleq {\arg\min _{\hat{G \in H \mathcal{L (\hat{G , \mathcal{D )$, where $\mathcal{L}$ is the Nash equilibrium loss of the GAN, and $H$ is the hypothesis space.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: We drop auxiliary notations like the discriminator.. Hallucination hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-047: A sample $(s, t) \in S \times T$ with $\hat{G (s)$ that ``conflicts'' with $t$ is termed a hallucination.}
elements.sent-2411-048: This is a necessary detail to define the problem but is ill-defined.
elements.sent-2411-049: While studies in inverse medical imaging \cite{bhadra2021hallucinations, tivnan2024hallucination put forth hallucination indexes, they are unsuitable herein due to their assumptions about data linearity and stochastic models.}
elements.sent-2411-050: In what follows, we use popular full-reference (FR)\footnote{No-reference (NR) compares $\hat{G (s)$ to $P_T$, {which cannot assay realistic hallucinations when $\hat{G (s) \in P_T$}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: For consistency, we use {1-LPIPS , ensuring $\mathcal{Q (\hat{G (s), t) \leqslant 1$, $\forall \mathcal{Q}$, with lower values indicating hallucinations.}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: A statistical analysis of these metrics on an SRS-to-HE VS experiment (from \S\ref{sec:5.1 ) showed that Kendall's $\tau$ \cite{kendall1938new is roughly 0.4-0.5 among one another, indicating moderate-to-strong rank consistency.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Note, pathologist evaluations are impractical for large-scale studies such as ours due to high cost and variability.
elements.sent-2411-055: Hallucinations stem from the rudimentary failure of $\hat{G}$ to emulate $G$, influenced by several factors delineated by the VS pipeline (Fig. \ref{fig2 ).}
elements.sent-2411-056: Briefly, we discuss them, while also providing an empirical demonstration of these factors in an SRS-to-HE VS experiment in Fig. \ref{fig2 .}
elements.sent-2411-057: This includes randomness in the generating process (e.g., measurement noise) or insufficient source domain content (e.g., low resolution).
elements.sent-2411-058: Such intrinsic ill-posedness leads to one-to-many translations for $G(s)$, where plausible translations may not match true observations, causing hallucinations.
elements.sent-2411-059: Even without these issues, {underspecification \cite{mehrer2020individual, d2022underspecification leads to infinite candidate solutions (aka Rashomon set \cite{breiman2001statistical , $H^* \subset H$) equivalently describing the data, where $\mathcal{L _{\text{val (\hat{G , \mathcal{D ) < \tau$, $\forall \hat{G \in H^*$, with $\mathcal{L _{\text{val }$ as a validation criteria, and $\tau$ a threshold.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: The realized solution from $H^*$ may not match $G$ as there is no theoretical favor towards it.
elements.sent-2411-061: This is pronounced in small datasets or under-constrained I2IT, notably unsupervised ones \cite{liu2017unsupervised, lu2019guiding, shen2020one .}
elements.sent-2411-062: This leads to {out-of-distribution (OOD) encounters where $s \notin P_S$ (cf., {in-distribution , ID, $s \in P_S$).}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Examples are institute or population shifts \cite{howard2021impact , or unseen artifacts or pathologies \cite{oh2024we .}}
elements.sent-2411-064: VS models are prone to hallucinate over OOD, a ramification of underspecification: ERM only constrains ID behavior, resulting many $H^*$ members exhibiting poor OOD generalization \cite{arjovsky2019invariant, d2022underspecification, teney2022predicting .}
elements.sent-2411-065: In addition, cybersecurity is highly relevant for digital pathology \cite{ghaffari2022adversarial .}
elements.sent-2411-066: Unfortunately, I2IT models lack adversarial robustness, with research showing that injecting imperceptible noise disrupts malicious applications like deepfake \cite{yeh2020disrupting, yang2021defending and watermark removers \cite{liu2022watermark .}}
elements.sent-2411-067: This hints that VS models too can be attacked with hallucination intent.
elements.sent-2411-068: Acknowledging these vast factors, we aim to construct a monitor $f: S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ returning the VS prediction confidence.
elements.sent-2411-069: This monitor should ideally correlate with $\mathcal{Q (\hat{G (s), t)$ and operate blindly, without prior knowledge of $t$.. Evaluation metric.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: In real world applications, monitors are deployed in binary form--accept if $f(s) \geqslant \lambda$ (a threshold), otherwise reject.
elements.sent-2411-071: An effective monitor should preferentially reject hallucinations to maintain the fidelity of accepted images.
elements.sent-2411-072: Inspired by \cite{malininensemble, van2020uncertainty, postels2022practicality , we evaluate hallucination detection performance as follows: For a test set $\mathcal{D _{\text{test }$, we set threshold $\lambda_{p}$ to reject $p\%$ of samples, satisfying $\text{Pr _{(s,t) \in \mathcal{D _{\text{test [f(s) \geqslant \lambda_p] = 1-p$, and compute the average hallucination magnitude of non-rejected samples.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: To sidestep the sensitivity of $p$, we sweep $p\in[0,1)$ and measure the area-under-curve (AUC): \begin{equation}AUC_{f} \triangleq \int^{1}_{0} \mathbb{E}_{(s,t) \in \mathcal{D}_{\text{test}} \, | \, f(s) \geqslant \lambda_p} \bigr[ \mathcal{Q}(\hat{G}(s), t)\bigr] dp. \label{eq1}\end{equation} While this metric reflects monitor performance, it is biased towards VS models with better base performance.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}AUC_{f} \triangleq \int^{1}_{0} E_{(s,t) \in D_{test} \, | \, f(s) \geqslant \lambda_p} \bigr[ Q(\hat{G}(s), t)\bigr] dp. \label{eq1}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-074: To adjust for this, we normalize w.r.t.
elements.sent-2411-075: The former assigns accept/reject decisions randomly, yielding an AUC equivalent to $\mathbb{E _{(s,t) \in \mathcal{D _{\text{test \bigr[ \mathcal{Q (\hat{G (s), t)\bigr]$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: The oracle's AUC represents the hypothetical theoretical upper bound, calculated using $\mathcal{Q (\hat{G (s), t)$ as the confidence.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: Our final metric, coined {Hallucination Rejection Preference (HRP) , is defined as: \begin{equation}HRP \triangleq \frac{(AUC_f - AUC_{\text{random}})}{(AUC_{\text{oracle}} - AUC_{\text{random}})}. \label{eq2}\end{equation} A higher HRP value approaching 1 indicates better monitor performance, computed separately for each $\mathcal{Q}$ metric.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Note, simultaneously achieving maximal (=1) HRP on all metrics is infeasible, as this would require the monitor to perfectly align with the rankings of multiple metrics, which is impossible due to Kendall's $\tau \neq 1$.
elements.sent-2411-079: OOD Detection The mainstream detection problem of OOD (including sub-categories like anomaly and novelty) focuses on differentiating OOD from ID \cite{yang2024generalized .}
elements.sent-2411-080: For instance, the mean MS-SSIM only dropped from 0.464 to 0.435 when switching from ID to OOD test data (Fig. \ref{fig2 ).}
elements.sent-2411-081: Fig. \ref{fig3 -c further illustrates the sample-dependent effects of these shifts, suggesting that {we should aim to detect just its fatal cases .}}
elements.sent-2411-082: Otherwise, we raise excessive flags and compromise the open-world applicability of vs. {(ii) ID is not hallucination-safe, as it can arise from domain-agnostic factors like data ambiguity, suboptimal training, Rashomon effect, and attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-083: This is supported by the significant overlap in MS-SSIM PDFs between ID and OOD test sets (Fig. \ref{fig2 ).}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig3.png} \caption{The data dependency of hallucinogenic factors. We plot the MS-SSIM per sample in Fig. \ref{fig2} before (x-axis) vs. after (y-axis) select ablations: (a) reducing SRS source content from 4 to 2 bands, (b) underspecification by switching Pix2PixHD with CycleGAN \cite{zhu2017unpaired}, (c) shifting the ID test data to become OOD.} \label{fig3}
elements.sent-2411-084: Note, similar arguments have been raised in image classification \cite{guerin2023out, jaegercall, averly2023unified, jundevil .}
elements.sent-2411-085: A key distinction though lies in semantic OOD (i.e., novelty).
elements.sent-2411-086: In classification, the clear-cut closed label set during training mandates the detection of semantic OODs without exception, which fall outside the classifier's scope.
elements.sent-2411-087: However, VS involves image generation, void of such constraints with outputs capable of expressing any image.
elements.sent-2411-088: A recent large-scale study on VS generalization \cite{tonks2024can proved this with novel phenotypes and cell types, suggesting that semantic OOD should be treated like other OOD types.}
elements.sent-2411-089: Furthermore, ID hallucinations might mislead one to think this is an outlier issue, which do not discern ID/OOD and simply refer to abnormalities w.r.t.
elements.sent-2411-090: However, as discussed, most hallucinogenic factors are distribution-agnostic, refuting this notion.
elements.sent-2411-091: We further validate this in Fig. \ref{fig4 .}

We visualize the Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP) \cite{mcinnes2018umap} of the VS model embeddings for ID. In addition to the SRS-to-HE experiment in Fig. \ref{fig2} (left), we also show a HO342-to-CD3 case from \S\ref{sec:5} (right). Color code reflects \textbf{\color{violet}{low}}/\textbf{\color{orange}{high}} MS-SSIM, with top hallucinatory instances (lowest MS-SSIM) marked by \textbf{\color{cyan}{$\star$}}. Note how outliers are not necessarily hallucinations and vice versa.} \label{fig4}
elements.sent-2411-092: Lastly, much of these discussions extend to other factors: hallucination detection is not merely an, e.g., ambiguity or underspecified problem (Fig. \ref{fig3 -a and -b).}

elements.sent-2411-094: Hence, hallucination detection (and its evaluation) should not focus solely on criteria latent to these factors, but instead align with VS predictions--{detecting if and only if $\mathcal{Q (\hat{G (s), t)$ is poor .}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: We present our NHP monitor leveraging embedded neural signals from the trained VS model, specifically the $k$\textsuperscript{th nearest neighbor (KNN) method, to score confidence.. Assumptions.}
elements.sent-2411-096: If permitted, it may be conveniently drawn from the training set, $\mathcal{D ^* \subseteq \mathcal{D}$.}
elements.sent-2411-097: We assume this henceforth as it applies to our study (recall, we focus on fully- and inconsistently-paired datasets).
elements.sent-2411-098: These assumptions are reasonable in clinical settings as the developer of the monitor and the VS model are usually the same.
elements.sent-2411-099: Let $s \in S$ be a sample for hallucination assessment.
elements.sent-2411-100: Denoting by $\hat{G ^{l (\cdot) \in \mathbb{R ^{C \times H \times W}$ the $l$-th layer CNN block of the VS model, we extract the feature vector, $z^{l \in \mathbb{R ^C$, with \begin{equation}\begin{split} z^{l}_{c} \triangleq \frac{1}{HW} \sum_h^H \sum_w^W \hat{G}^{l}_{c,h,w}(s), \forall c \in \{1,2,\dots,C\}. \end{split} \label{eq3}\end{equation} Our confidence scoring function utilizes two feature signals: the {(i) feature norm (FN) , computed by the $\ell_2$ norm: \begin{equation}{FN} \triangleq || z^l ||_{2}, \label{eq4}\end{equation} and the {(ii) KNN feature distance to a bank of ``safe'' samples in $\mathcal{D ^*$, devoid of hallucinations.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: We define this subset based on a truncation intensity $q\in[0,1)$: \begin{equation}\mathcal{D}^*_{q} \triangleq \bigl\{ (s^*, t^*) \in \mathcal{D}^* \, \big| \, \mathcal{Q}(\hat{G}(s^*), t^*) \geqslant \lambda_{\mathcal{Q}, q}, \forall \mathcal{Q} \bigl\}, \label{eq5}\end{equation} where, for each $\mathcal{Q}$ metric, the threshold $\lambda_{\mathcal{Q , q}$ satisfies $\text{Pr _{(s^*, t^*) \in \mathcal{D ^* [\mathcal{Q (\hat{G (s^*), t^*) \geqslant \lambda_{\mathcal{Q , q ] = 1-q$.}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}D^*_{q} \triangleq \bigl\{ (s^*, t^*) \in D^* \, \big| \, Q(\hat{G}(s^*), t^*) \geqslant \lambda_{Q, q}, \forall Q \bigl\}, \label{eq5}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-102: It holds that $\mathcal{D ^*_{q \subseteq \mathcal{D ^*$, with equality at $q=0$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-103: Letting ${Z^*_q ^{l}$ be the feature set of samples in $\mathcal{D ^*_q$, we aggregate normalized Euclidean distances between $z^l$ and ${z^* ^l \in {Z^*_q ^{l}$ as follows: \begin{equation}r(z^l; {Z^*_q}^{l}) \triangleq \Bigl\{ \big\| \tfrac{z^l}{||z^l||_2} - \tfrac{{z^*}^l}{||{z^*}^l||_2} \big\|_2 \, \big| \, \forall {z^*}^l \in {Z^*_q}^{l} \Bigl\}. \label{eq6}\end{equation} The normalization helps decouple FN.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-104: We reorder this set in ascending order, denoting by $\{{r_{(i) (z^l; {Z^*_q ^{l )}\}_{i=1 ^{n}$ such that the reordered index satisfy $r_{(1) (z^l; {Z^*_q ^{l ) \leqslant {r_{(2) (z^l; {Z^*_q ^{l ) \leqslant \dots \leqslant {r_{(n) (z^l; {Z^*_q ^{l )}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-105: We take the negative of the $k$\textsuperscript{th index: \begin{equation}{KNN} \triangleq -r_{(k)}(z^l; {Z^*_q}^{l}). \label{eq7}\end{equation} Finally, we integrate these signals to form our confidence scoring function: \begin{equation}f_{\text{NHP}}(s; \hat{G}, \mathcal{D}^*, l, q, k, \gamma) \triangleq {KNN} \times {FN}^{\gamma}, \label{eq8}\end{equation} where $\gamma$ acts as a balance coefficient.}
elements.sent-2411-106: With this configuration, we certify lower confidence (higher hallucination threat) when a sample's feature is distant from the non-hallucinatory portion of $\mathcal{D ^*$, adjusted by FN.}
elements.sent-2411-107: Apart from $\hat{G}$ and $\mathcal{D ^*$, we rely on four parameters: $\{l, q, k, \gamma\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-108: To tune these, we reserve a portion of $\mathcal{D ^*$ for validation and perform a grid search to maximize average HRP on this held-out subset.}
elements.sent-2411-109: Note, hallucinations are not removed (as per Eq. \ref{eq5 ) for this set.}
elements.sent-2411-110: We call this method ``self-tuning'' as $\mathcal{D ^*$ is subsampled from the training set in our work.}
elements.sent-2411-111: Self-tuning essentially relies on the hypothesis that a monitor effective over the training set holds across unseen data.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig5.png} \caption{Schematic pipeline of NHP. The hallucination-free feature bank and NHP parameters are determined beforehand using a calibration dataset, which may be sampled from the training set. During VS inference, we extract the feature, compute FN and KNN, and balance them per Eq. \ref{eq8} to estimate the VS prediction confidence.} \label{fig5}
elements.sent-2411-113: NHP adopts the KNN-based detector in \cite{sun2022out , recovering the vanilla formulation when setting $l$ to the penultimate layer and $q=\gamma=0$.}
elements.sent-2411-114: These design choices are well-justified for semantic OOD detection in image classifiers.
elements.sent-2411-115: For instance, empirical studies show that deeper layers reflect image semantics \cite{sun2022out, park2023nearest, lee2018simple, gomesigeood, wang2022vim, henzinger2020outside, van2020uncertainty , with ID data exhibiting higher FN values \cite{dhamija2018reducing, tack2020csi, sun2022out, yu2023block, park2023understanding , prompting the need to decouple it ($\gamma=0$) to maintain higher confidence for ID, as per Eq. \ref{eq7 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-116: Under the ID/OOD dichotomy, it also makes sense to use the entire $\mathcal{D ^*$ ($q=0$) as all samples are unequivocally ID when drawn from training.}
elements.sent-2411-117: However, we speculate that these choices are suboptimal for VS due to contextual differences in task type ({image classification vs. I2IT ) and detection goal ({OOD vs. hallucinations , \S\ref{sec:3.4 ).}}}

elements.sent-2411-131: We verify NHP's effectiveness in detecting hallucinations across various VS settings, described below.. Datasets.
elements.sent-2411-132: We consider seven VS tasks: (i) In-house SRS data in prostate cancer (PCa) translating to HE.
elements.sent-2411-133: We conduct SRS imaging at four Raman shift frequencies of 2847, 2879, 2933, and 2979 cm\textsuperscript{-1 , corresponding to four image channels.}
elements.sent-2411-134: Afterwards, the tissue sections were HE-stained and digitized at 20x magnification using Nanozoomer scanners.
elements.sent-2411-135: The SRS and HE WSIs were registered, split by patient for training/testing, and divided into patches.
elements.sent-2411-136: This dataset was acquired in collaboration with Mayo Clinic, Rochester, MN, with full details in \cite{falahkheirkhah2023accelerating .}
elements.sent-2411-137: The SRS and HO342 datasets are paired, while MIST is inconsistently paired.
elements.sent-2411-138: All experiments use 256×256 pixel\textsuperscript{2 patches (tessellated for MIST, which is in 1024×1024 pixel\textsuperscript{2 ), resulting in approximately 4K, 404K, and 69K training samples for SRS, HO342, MIST, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-139: These datasets cover diverse cancer (PCa, BC, ccRCC), dataset sizes (4-475K), and imaging modalities (HE, IHCs, IFs, label-free SRS).. I2IT methods.
elements.sent-2411-140: For paired datasets, we consider the supervised (i) Pix2PixHD \cite{wang2018high , and advanced techniques like the VSGD \cite{liu2023vsgd model with the PatchNCE \cite{andonian2021contrastive loss, which we term (ii) VSGD+pNCE.}}}
elements.sent-2411-141: For inconsistently paired datasets, we assess two distinct unsupervised methods: (iii) CycleGAN \cite{zhu2017unpaired and (iv) CUT \cite{park2020contrastive .}}
elements.sent-2411-142: These amount to 7×2 VS settings, each run 10 times with different seeds, totaling 140 VS models.
elements.sent-2411-144: For SRS, all models are trained with a batch size of 8 and a learning rate of 0.002 for 30 epochs, linearly decaying to zero over the last 15 epochs.
elements.sent-2411-145: For HO342, all models use a learning rate of 0.0002, batch size of 8, and train for 1 epoch.
elements.sent-2411-146: On MIST, all models train with a batch size of 1, learning rate of 0.0002, and 10 epochs.. Evaluation metrics.
elements.sent-2411-147: Over the test set, we independently compute the HRP using PSNR, MS-SSIM, and LPIPS, but report their mean due to space constraints.. NHP implementation details.
elements.sent-2411-148: For grid search, we explore: (i) $l\in\{0,0.25,0.5,0.75,1\}$, where indexes denote the depth ratio, e.g., 0 is the first layer coinciding to the shallowest feature, whereas 1 is the penultimate layer; (ii) $q \in \{0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75\}$; (iii) $k\in\{1,10,25,50,100,200\}$; (iv) $\gamma \in [-10,10]$ in steps of 0.5.
elements.sent-2411-149: For $\mathcal{D ^*$, we use the entire SRS training set and stratified 1\% and 10\% subsets for HO342 and MIST, respectively, yielding approximately 3-7K samples for every experiment.}
elements.sent-2411-150: Each 140 VS run evaluates NHP 10 times with different validation splits (25\%) for self-tuning, totaling 1400 NHP counts.
elements.sent-2411-152: We compare with GAN-based anomaly detection methods, the basis of recent VS hallucination detection studies \cite{huang2024autonomous, ounissi2024scalable : (i) ALOCC \cite{sabokrou2018adversarially, zaheer2020old uses the output of the trained target domain discriminator.}}
elements.sent-2411-153: Without loss of generality, we denote this discriminator(s) by $\hat{D _T = \{\hat{D _{T,1 , \hat{D _{T,2 ,\dots,\hat{D _{T,N}\}$, and define confidence by $\frac{1 {N \sum_{n=1 ^{N \hat{D _{T,n (\hat{G (s))$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-154: Let $\hat{F :T\rightarrow S$ and $\hat{D _{S}$ denote the target-to-source model and source domain discriminator, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-155: Confidence is computed by the feature matching loss between $s$ and $\hat{s = \hat{F (\hat{G (s))$, specifically $-\big\| \hat{D _S^{(-1) (s) - \hat{D _S^{(-1) (\hat{s ) \big\|_{2}$ where $\hat{D _S^{(-1)}$ denotes the penultimate layer feature extractor of $\hat{D _S$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-156: We also evaluate ablation versions of NHP: (iv) $\gamma=0$, ignoring FN; (v) $q=0$, with no data removal in $\mathcal{D ^*$; (vi) the linear balance of $\text{KNN + \gamma\text{FN}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-157: All unspecified parameters are tuned in the same manner.
elements.sent-2411-158: We also substitute KNN with alternate ``distance'' measures: (vii) Outside-the-Box (OtB, without K-means) \cite{henzinger2020outside constructs a $C$-dimensional box (i.e., hyperrectangle) based on the $p$-th percentile calibration feature elements in ${Z^*_q ^{l}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-159: Afterwards, for a sample $s$ with feature $z\in\mathbb{R ^C$, we replace Eq. \ref{eq7 with the ratio of in-box feature elements.}}
elements.sent-2411-160: We then use the negative $\ell_1$ norm of $z$ projected onto the residual subspace orthogonal to this subspace.
elements.sent-2411-161: For all three variants, we appropriately scale the scores before merging with FN.
elements.sent-2411-162: For fair comparison, we perform grid searches to tune respective parameters as well: $p\in\{1, 0.99, 0.975, 0.95, 0.9, 0.8\}$ for OtB, $r\in\{0.99, 0.975, 0.95, 0.925, 0.9, 0.8\}$ for Residual, and $c_k\in\{1,4,8,16,32,64\}$ for GMM.

elements.sent-2411-191: For OOD, we simulate clinically relevant corruptions to the test set while preserving pathological content.
elements.sent-2411-192: For SRS, we consider six types--(i) additive noise with variable intensity, (ii) contrast jitters, (iii) blurs using a defocus, motion, or zoom kernels, (iv) pixel saturation by setting random small boxes to the maximum value, (v) pixel dropout, and (vi) misregistration by slightly offseting the alignment between channels via affine transformation.
elements.sent-2411-193: These reflect real-world acquisition errors such as instrument failures.
elements.sent-2411-194: For HO342, we similarly apply (i) noise, (ii) blurs, (iii) contrast jitters, and (iv) pixel dropout.
elements.sent-2411-195: For MIST, we consider popular HE corruptions in literature--(i) image compression to JPEG or WebP, (ii) stain color variations, (iii) blurs, and (d) artifacts by superimposing a marker or bubble mask (from \cite{zhang2022benchmarking ).}

elements.sent-2411-196: For adversarial examples, we apply Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) \cite{madry2018towards to iteratively perturb test sample $s$ into $s^{\text{adv }$, maximizing the error between $\hat{G (s^{\text{adv )$ and $t$ (or $\hat{G (s)$ if $t$ is unknown to the attacker).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-197: Denoting the $t$-th step as $s^{\text{adv _{t}$ with $s^{\text{adv _{0 =s$, we update as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{split} s^{\text{adv}}_{t+1} = \Pi \Bigl( s^{\text{adv}}_{t} + \alpha \cdot \text{sign}\bigl(\nabla_s(||\hat{G}(s^{\text{adv}}_t) - \xi||_2^2)\bigl)\Bigl), s.t. \quad \xi \in \{t, \hat{G}(s)\}, \, ||s^{\text{adv}}_{t+1} - s||_{p} \leqslant \epsilon, \end{split} \label{eq9}\end{equation} where $\epsilon$ is the perturbation budget, $\alpha$ is the step size, $\nabla_s(\cdot)$ is the gradient w.r.t.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-198: While other losses can be used, the pixel-wise $\ell_2$-norm has proven effective (see Fig. \ref{fig8 ).}
elements.sent-2411-199: We generate diverse examples by randomly drawing $\epsilon \in \{1/255, 4/255, 8/255\}$, $p\in\{2,\infty\}$, and $\xi \in \{t, \hat{G (s)\}$, per test sample.}
elements.sent-2411-200: The step size, $\alpha$, is fixed at 0.2 for $\ell_2$ and 1/255 for $\ell_\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-201: We use $t=10$ PGD steps for HO342 and MIST, and $t=50$ for SRS, which we observed to be more resilient.

elements.sent-2411-207: In this section, we investigate whether VS models with higher average performance (AP), $\mathbb{E _{(s,t)\in\mathcal{D _{\text{test [\mathcal{Q (\hat{G (s),t)]$, enable better hallucination detection.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-208: This is crucial because VS literature reports only AP, whereas real-world deployment hinges on multiple safety measures \cite{hendrycks2021unsolved , {specifically the ability to both withstand and identify hallucinations .}}
elements.sent-2411-209: Having established NHP as a strong baseline, we use it as our monitor backbone herein.. Setup.
elements.sent-2411-210: Across our 20 models per dataset, we compare six measures: AP and HRP for test, OOD, and adversarial sets.
elements.sent-2411-211: Each $\mathcal{Q}$ metric is analyzed separately, as AP scores cannot be averaged like HRP due to scale differences.

elements.sent-2411-226: In this work, we studied hallucination detection for VS, contributing in mainly three dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-227: First, we coherently established the problem with a summary of hallucination causes, an evaluation metric, and clarification with related detection tasks.
elements.sent-2411-228: Second, we introduced a novel hallucination detection method, NHP, with extensive validation.
elements.sent-2411-229: Third, we revealed new insights, particularly on the evaluation of VS robustness.
elements.sent-2411-230: Together, as the first work of its kind, this work provides a useful framework to assess hallucinations in VS images that could prove useful across the biomedical research and clinical landscape.. \nocite{* \bibliographystyle{ieeetr.}}
